title: Cat Black Tarry Discharge from Vulva: Causes & Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Black tarry discharge from a cat's vulva is a serious veterinary emergency. Learn what it means and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
What is black tarry discharge from the vulva?

Black or tarry discharge from a cat's vulva is typically a sign of necrotic (dying) tissue, old blood, or a severe infection within the reproductive tract. In many cases, this is associated with a life-threatening uterine infection called pyometra, or complications related to pregnancy such as a retained fetus or placenta that has begun to decompose. This is not a normal finding in healthy cats and requires professional medical intervention.

Is it a veterinary emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for this symptom is High. Because black discharge often indicates decomposition or systemic infection, your cat is at high risk for sepsis, which can be fatal if not treated within hours. If your cat is also lethargic, refusing food, or has a swollen abdomen, you should transport them to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

Why a photo helps with triage

When you call an emergency vet, providing a clear photo of the discharge and the vulva can be incredibly helpful. A photo allows the triage team to assess the volume, consistency, and exact color of the fluid. This visual information helps them determine the severity of the situation and prepare the necessary surgical or stabilization equipment before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Is black discharge normal after a cat gives birth?
faqs.answer: No. While dark green or reddish-brown discharge can be normal for a short period after labor, a black, tarry, or foul-smelling discharge suggests a retained placenta or infection and requires immediate veterinary care.

faqs.question: What is Pyometra?
faqs.answer: Pyometra is a life-threatening infection of the uterus that occurs most commonly in unspayed female cats. It causes the uterus to fill with pus and toxins, which can leak into the bloodstream.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because black discharge often indicates tissue death or severe infection, waiting even a few hours can lead to a ruptured uterus or systemic shock. Emergency care is recommended.
